content:
TOPIC: A clinical assessment is required for a patient presenting with the following symptoms. 

FACTS:
A 41-year-old male with type-2 diabetes, hypertension, and IHD presented to the emergency department one week after ureteroscopy and laser lithotripsy for a right-sided mid-ureteric stone. 
The patient had complaints of severe back pain, nausea, and vomiting. The pain was radiating to both lower limbs and was associated with significant weakness in both legs. 
The patient reported no history of trauma, fever, dysuria, or hematuria. 
The patient’s vital signs were as follows: heart rate 130/min, blood pressure 150/90 mmHg, O2 saturation 98%, respiratory rate 20/min, and temperature 36 C. 
Both lower limbs were dusky in color; bilateral peripheral pulses of the femoral, popliteal, and dorsalis pedis arteries were absent. 
An echocardiogram (ECG) showed sinus  tachycardia  with  evidence  of  previous  anterior ischemic changes. 
At this time, lower limb ischemia was strongly suspected, and an urgent computed tomography angiography (CTA) of the chest, abdomen, and lower limbs was performed. 
Laboratory results showed a normal CBC of Hb 15.3 g/dL (ref. value: 12–17.5 g/dL), WBC 4.6 x109/L (ref. value: 4-10 x109/L) and platelet count 287 x109/L (ref. value: 150-410  x109/L). 
The  chemistry  panel  showed  normal electrolytes with mildly elevated creatinine 109 umol/L (ref. value: 44-115 umol/L). 
Creatine phosphokinase (CPK) and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) enzymes were markedly elevated. 
CPK level was 58,155 IU/L (ref. value: 22-200 IU/L) while LDH level was 1,793 IU/L (ref. value 140-280 IU/L). 
CTA showed that the distal part of the abdominal aorta was totally occluded, with a massive intramural thrombus that extended distally to both iliac arteries. 
Further thrombosis was detected in both the renal and splenic arteries, resulting in a subtotal infarction of the right kidney, a segmental infarction of the left renal lower pole, and a wedge-shaped infarction of the mid-part of the spleen. 
Notably, the scan did not reveal any evidence of dissection in any aortic division.

The patient presents with acute bilateral lower limb ischemia secondary to distal abdominal aortic occlusion by a massive thrombus extending into both iliac arteries. The associated thrombosis in renal and splenic arteries has caused subtotal right kidney infarction and segmental infarction of the left renal lower pole, along with splenic infarction. Elevated CPK and LDH indicate significant muscle ischemia and tissue injury. Immediate vascular intervention is critical to restore limb perfusion and prevent further organ damage. Renal function should be closely monitored given the infarctions and mildly elevated creatinine. Supportive care including anticoagulation and management of underlying risk factors (diabetes, hypertension, IHD) is essential.

TOPIC: A clinical assessment is required for a patient presenting with the following symptoms. 

FACTS:
A 41-year-old male with type-2 diabetes, hypertension, and IHD presented to the emergency department one week after ureteroscopy and laser lithotripsy for a right-sided mid-ureteric stone. 
The patient had complaints of severe back pain, nausea, and vomiting. The pain was radiating to both lower limbs and was associated with significant weakness in both legs. 
The patient reported no history of trauma, fever, dysuria, or hematuria. 
The patient’s vital signs were as follows: heart rate 130/min, blood pressure 150/90 mmHg, O2 saturation 98%, respiratory rate 20/min, and temperature 36 C. 
Both lower limbs were dusky in color; bilateral peripheral pulses of the femoral, popliteal, and dorsalis pedis arteries were absent. 
An echocardiogram (ECG) showed sinus  tachycardia  with  evidence  of  previous  anterior ischemic changes. 
At this time, lower limb ischemia was strongly suspected, and an urgent computed tomography angiography (CTA) of the chest, abdomen, and lower limbs was performed. 
Laboratory results showed a normal CBC of Hb 15.3 g/dL (ref. value: 12–17.5 g/dL), WBC 4.6 x109/L (ref. value: 4-10 x109/L) and platelet count 287 x109/L (ref. value: 150-410  x109/L). 
The  chemistry  panel  showed  normal electrolytes with mildly elevated creatinine 109 umol/L (ref. value: 44-115 umol/L). 
Creatine phosphokinase (CPK) and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) enzymes were markedly elevated. 
CPK level was 58,155 IU/L (ref. value: 22-200 IU/L) while LDH level was 1,793 IU/L (ref. value 140-280 IU/L). 
CTA showed that the distal part of the abdominal aorta was totally occluded, with a massive intramural thrombus that extended distally to both iliac arteries. 
Further thrombosis was detected in both the renal and splenic arteries, resulting in a subtotal infarction of the right kidney, a segmental infarction of the left renal lower pole, and a wedge-shaped infarction of the mid-part of the spleen. 
Notably, the scan did not reveal any evidence of dissection in any aortic division.

The patient presents with acute extensive arterial thrombosis involving the distal abdominal aorta, bilateral iliac arteries, renal, and splenic arteries, causing multiorgan infarctions and bilateral lower limb ischemia. Immediate endovascular intervention with catheter-directed thrombolysis and possible angioplasty/stenting is indicated to restore perfusion and prevent further ischemic damage. Given the absence of aortic dissection, the thrombotic occlusion is likely embolic or thrombotic in origin, possibly related to recent surgery and underlying vascular disease. Urgent multidisciplinary management including vascular surgery consultation is essential.
Please provide urgent risk assessment and treatment plan. Time critical!

Urgent risk assessment: The patient has acute bilateral lower limb ischemia due to distal abdominal aortic occlusion by massive thrombus extending into both iliac arteries, with associated renal artery thrombosis. This poses high risk of limb loss, renal failure, and systemic embolism.

Treatment plan:
1. Immediate systemic anticoagulation with IV heparin to prevent thrombus propagation.
2. Urgent vascular surgery or endovascular intervention (thrombectomy/thrombolysis) to restore limb and renal perfusion.
3. Monitor renal function closely; prepare for possible renal replacement therapy if acute kidney injury develops.
4. Supportive care: maintain hemodynamics, hydration, and monitor for reperfusion injury.
5. Multidisciplinary coordination with vascular surgery, nephrology, and critical care teams.

Urgent risk assessment: High risk of multiorgan failure due to extensive arterial thrombosis involving distal abdominal aorta, bilateral iliac, renal, and splenic arteries. No aortic dissection noted, which simplifies intervention approach.

Treatment plan:
1. Immediate systemic anticoagulation with IV heparin to prevent further thrombus propagation.
2. Urgent catheter-directed thrombolysis targeting distal abdominal aorta and bilateral iliac arteries to restore perfusion.
3. Consider percutaneous mechanical thrombectomy if thrombolysis is insufficient or contraindicated.
4. Angioplasty and stent placement as needed to maintain vessel patency post-thrombolysis.
5. Monitor renal function closely; consider nephrology consult.
6. Supportive care for organ infarctions; ICU monitoring essential.

Time is critical—initiate anticoagulation and plan endovascular intervention immediately.

TOPIC: A clinical assessment is required for a patient presenting with the following symptoms. 

FACTS:
A 41-year-old male with type-2 diabetes, hypertension, and IHD presented to the emergency department one week after ureteroscopy and laser lithotripsy for a right-sided mid-ureteric stone. 
The patient had complaints of severe back pain, nausea, and vomiting. The pain was radiating to both lower limbs and was associated with significant weakness in both legs. 
The patient reported no history of trauma, fever, dysuria, or hematuria. 
The patient’s vital signs were as follows: heart rate 130/min, blood pressure 150/90 mmHg, O2 saturation 98%, respiratory rate 20/min, and temperature 36 C. 
Both lower limbs were dusky in color; bilateral peripheral pulses of the femoral, popliteal, and dorsalis pedis arteries were absent. 
An echocardiogram (ECG) showed sinus  tachycardia  with  evidence  of  previous  anterior ischemic changes. 
At this time, lower limb ischemia was strongly suspected, and an urgent computed tomography angiography (CTA) of the chest, abdomen, and lower limbs was performed. 
Laboratory results showed a normal CBC of Hb 15.3 g/dL (ref. value: 12–17.5 g/dL), WBC 4.6 x109/L (ref. value: 4-10 x109/L) and platelet count 287 x109/L (ref. value: 150-410  x109/L). 
The  chemistry  panel  showed  normal electrolytes with mildly elevated creatinine 109 umol/L (ref. value: 44-115 umol/L). 
Creatine phosphokinase (CPK) and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) enzymes were markedly elevated. 
CPK level was 58,155 IU/L (ref. value: 22-200 IU/L) while LDH level was 1,793 IU/L (ref. value 140-280 IU/L). 
CTA showed that the distal part of the abdominal aorta was totally occluded, with a massive intramural thrombus that extended distally to both iliac arteries. 
Further thrombosis was detected in both the renal and splenic arteries, resulting in a subtotal infarction of the right kidney, a segmental infarction of the left renal lower pole, and a wedge-shaped infarction of the mid-part of the spleen. 
Notably, the scan did not reveal any evidence of dissection in any aortic division.

The patient presents with acute extensive arterial thrombosis involving the distal abdominal aorta, bilateral iliac arteries, renal, and splenic arteries, causing multiorgan infarctions and bilateral lower limb ischemia. Immediate endovascular intervention with catheter-directed thrombolysis and possible angioplasty/stenting is indicated to restore perfusion and prevent further ischemic damage. Given the absence of aortic dissection, the thrombotic occlusion is likely embolic or thrombotic in origin, possibly related to recent surgery and underlying vascular disease. Urgent multidisciplinary management including vascular surgery consultation is essential.

Urgent risk assessment: The patient has acute bilateral lower limb ischemia due to distal abdominal aortic occlusion by massive thrombus extending into both iliac arteries, with associated renal artery thrombosis. This poses high risk of limb loss, renal failure, and systemic embolism.

Treatment plan:
1. Immediate systemic anticoagulation with IV heparin to prevent thrombus propagation.
2. Urgent vascular surgery or endovascular intervention (thrombectomy/thrombolysis) to restore limb and renal perfusion.
3. Monitor renal function closely; prepare for possible renal replacement therapy if acute kidney injury develops.
4. Supportive care: maintain hemodynamics, hydration, and monitor for reperfusion injury.
5. Multidisciplinary coordination with vascular surgery, nephrology, and critical care teams.

Urgent risk assessment: High risk of multiorgan failure due to extensive arterial thrombosis involving distal abdominal aorta, bilateral iliac, renal, and splenic arteries. No aortic dissection noted, which simplifies intervention approach.

Treatment plan:
1. Immediate systemic anticoagulation with IV heparin to prevent further thrombus propagation.
2. Urgent catheter-directed thrombolysis targeting distal abdominal aorta and bilateral iliac arteries to restore perfusion.
3. Consider percutaneous mechanical thrombectomy if thrombolysis is insufficient or contraindicated.
4. Angioplasty and stent placement as needed to maintain vessel patency post-thrombolysis.
5. Monitor renal function closely; consider nephrology consult.
6. Supportive care for organ infarctions; ICU monitoring essential.

Time is critical—initiate anticoagulation and plan endovascular intervention immediately.
